did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used machine learning (a subset of AI) to classify dermatological images. Specifically, it extracted statistical features from the HAM10000 dataset using the Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) method and trained five machine learning models: Support Vector Machine (SVM), Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), K-Nearest Neighbour (KNN), Logistic Regression (LR), and Neural Network (NN). The task was to distinguish between benign keratoses and dermatofibroma.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the selection of 200 images from the HAM10000 dataset, feature extraction using GLCM, and the training and assessment of five machine learning models (SVM, LDA, KNN, LR, NN). It reports specific quantitative results (e.g., LR accuracy of 91.50%) and conclusions derived from these new experiments, rather than summarizing prior literature.

title: Advancing Dermatological Image Classification: GLCM-Based Machine Learning Insights
journal: Advance Sustainable Science, Engineering and Technology (ASSET)
brief_summary: This study aimed to improve the classification of benign keratoses and dermatofibroma using machine learning on the HAM10000 dataset. Researchers selected 200 randomly chosen images (100 of each class) and resized them to 400x400 pixels for analysis. Statistical features were extracted using the Gray Level Co-occurrence Matrix (GLCM) with varying distances (1, 5, 10, 15) and angles (0°, 45°, 90°, 135°). Five machine learning models—SVM, LDA, KNN, Logistic Regression (LR), and Neural Network (NN)—were trained and evaluated based on accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score, and AUC. The Logistic Regression model achieved the highest performance with an accuracy of 91.50%, sensitivity of 93.00%, and F1-score of 91.36% when using a distance of 1 and an angle of 135°. The results indicate that GLCM-based feature extraction combined with LR is an effective strategy for distinguishing these specific skin diseases.

lesion_types_studied.details: One of the two classes used in the binary classification experiment. 100 images were selected from the HAM10000 dataset for this class. The paper notes these are commonly referred to as seborrheic keratoses.
One of the two classes used in the binary classification experiment. 100 images were selected from the HAM10000 dataset for this class.

all_task_types.target: Binary classification between benign keratoses and dermatofibroma
all_task_types.details: The paper performs binary classification to distinguish between two specific skin disease classes: benign keratoses and dermatofibroma. Five machine learning models (SVM, LDA, KNN, LR, NN) were trained on GLCM-extracted features from the HAM10000 dataset. Performance was evaluated using classification metrics including accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score, and AUC.

datasets_used.name: HAM10000
datasets_used.description: A public dermatological image dataset (Skin Cancer MNIST: HAM10000) obtained from Kaggle, originally containing 10,000 training images for comparing human and machine performance in skin disease classification. The study used a subset of 200 randomly selected images resized to 400x400 pixels.

use.training: The 200 selected images were used to train five machine learning models (SVM, LDA, KNN, LR, NN) after feature extraction via GLCM.
use.other: The dataset was sourced from Kaggle (https://www.kaggle.com/datasets/kmader/skin-cancer-mnist-ham10000).

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report any formal hypothesis testing, p-values, or significance tests. Performance was evaluated using descriptive metrics (accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, precision, F1-score, AUC) without statistical inference.

HAM10000.notes: The study found that the Logistic Regression model achieved the highest overall performance in terms of accuracy, sensitivity, and F1-score. The KNN model achieved the highest specificity and precision. The LDA model achieved the highest AUC. The angle of 135° and distance of 1 generally yielded the most optimal results across most metrics.

architecture_used.main_model: Five traditional machine learning models: Support Vector Machine (SVM), Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA), K-Nearest Neighbour (KNN), Logistic Regression (LR), and Neural Network (NN).

training_details.dataset: HAM10000 (subset of 200 images: 100 benign keratoses, 100 dermatofibroma)
training_details.hyperparameters: GLCM distance and angle values were varied to evaluate performance; specific model hyperparameters (e.g., kernel type for SVM, number of neighbors for KNN) were not reported.

preprocessing_techniques: Image resizing to 400x400 pixels to standardize input and reduce processing time
conclusions: The Logistic Regression (LR) model achieved the highest overall performance for classifying benign keratoses and dermatofibroma, with an accuracy of 91.50%, sensitivity of 93.00%, and F1-score of 91.36%.
The optimal GLCM feature extraction configuration for the Logistic Regression model was a distance of 1 and an angle of 135°.
The K-Nearest Neighbour (KNN) model achieved the highest specificity (97.00%) and precision (96.20%) when using a GLCM distance of 10 and an angle of 0°.
The Linear Discriminant Analysis (LDA) model achieved the highest Area Under the Curve (AUC) of 0.95 when using a GLCM distance of 1 and an angle of 135°.
GLCM-based feature extraction combined with machine learning models is an effective strategy for distinguishing between benign keratoses and dermatofibroma.
The study highlights the potential of AI and machine learning to automate diagnostics, reduce healthcare costs, and improve accessibility, particularly in underserved areas.
important_remarks: The study uses an extremely small subset of the HAM10000 dataset (only 200 images total: 100 benign keratoses and 100 dermatofibroma), which limits the statistical power and generalizability of the results.

The paper does not report a separate validation or testing split; it states that 200 images were 'randomly chosen' and used for training, raising significant concerns about data leakage and overfitting given the small sample size.
No external validation was performed; the models were evaluated solely on the internal subset of the HAM10000 dataset.

The paper does not report standard deviations, confidence intervals, or p-values for the performance metrics, making it impossible to assess the statistical significance of the differences between models.
Specific hyperparameters for the machine learning models (e.g., kernel type for SVM, number of neighbors for KNN, learning rate for NN) were not reported, hindering reproducibility.
The dataset selection was described as 'randomly chosen' without specifying the random seed or stratification method, which may introduce selection bias.

The study focuses only on two specific classes (benign keratoses and dermatofibroma) from the HAM10000 dataset, ignoring the other seven classes present in the original dataset, which limits the clinical relevance for broader skin cancer screening.
The paper claims the Logistic Regression model 'exceeded expected results' but does not define what these expectations were or provide a baseline for comparison.
The implementation was done in MATLAB, but the specific versions of the software or toolboxes used are not specified.
